title: Psalms 75
subtitle: We Praise You, God; Your Name Is Near

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “It Is I Who Judge with Equity”

heb.paragraph: "You say, ‘I choose the appointed time (mōʿēd); it is I who judge with equity’" (v.2). God speaks directly: HE chooses when to judge. When the earth quakes, HE holds its pillars firm (v.3). The warning to the arrogant: "do not lift your horns against heaven" (v.5). God’s timing is sovereign; human posturing is futile.

notes.note: "We praise you, God; your Name is near; people tell of your wonderful deeds." Nearness and narrative: God’s name is close; people tell his stories.

"I choose the appointed time; it is I who judge with equity. When the earth and all its people quake, it is I who hold its pillars firm." Divine first-person speech: timing, judgment, and cosmic stability all belong to God.
"To the arrogant I say, ‘Boast no more,’ and to the wicked, ‘Do not lift your horns.’" The horn is a symbol of power. God commands: lower your horns. Do not posture against heaven.

refs.note: God setting up and deposing kings parallels the psalm's divine appointment of judgment's timing.
Times and dates the Father has set by his own authority echoes the psalm's 'at the time I choose.'
Hannah's song of reversals — bringing low and exalting — shares the psalm's theology.

hist.context: Psalm 75 is an Asaphite liturgy combining thanksgiving (v.1), divine oracle (vv.2-5), and prophetic declaration (vv.6-10). God speaks directly: 'It is I who judge with equity' (v.2). The divine 'I' sections distinguish this psalm from typical human prayers. The earth 'and all who live in it quake' when God arises to judge.

heb.paragraph: "In the hand of the LORD is a cup (kōs) full of foaming wine mixed with spices; he pours it out, and all the wicked of the earth drink it down to its very dregs" (v.8). The cup of judgment: not a sip but drain it to the dregs. The image returns in Revelation 14:10. The psalm closes: "I will cut off the horns of all the wicked, but the horns of the righteous will be lifted up" (v.10). Horns lowered and raised by divine decree.

notes.note: "In the hand of the LORD is a cup full of foaming wine; all the wicked drink it to its dregs." The cup of divine wrath. Jesus in Gethsemane asked to let this cup pass (Matt 26:39). He drank what the wicked deserved.
"I will cut off the horns of the wicked, but the horns of the righteous will be lifted up." The reversal: arrogant power removed, humble power elevated. God adjusts the world’s power dynamics.

refs.note: Drinking the wine of God's fury poured full strength echoes the psalm's cup of foaming wine.
The cup of God's wrath that nations must drink develops the same judgment imagery.
Jesus praying about the cup he must drink transforms the judgment cup into atonement.

hist.context: The cup of judgment (v.8) that the wicked must 'drink down to its dregs' was a standard prophetic image for divine wrath. The warning 'do not lift your horns' (vv.4-5) addresses arrogant powers who exalt themselves. The psalm assures that promotion comes from neither east nor west but from God alone — a direct challenge to military and political presumption.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " Nearness and narrative: God’s name is close…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIt is God who judges: He brings one down, he exalts another.ESVbut it is God who executes judgment, putting down one and lifting up another.NIVWhen the earth and all its people quake, it is I who hold its pillars firm.ESVWhen the earth totters, and all its inhabitants, it is I who keep steady its pillars. Selah

rec.text: ""We praise you, God; your Name is near; people tell of your wonderful deeds." Nearness and narrative: God’s name is close; people tell his stories."

rows.label: The Cup — 75:6–10
rows.text: "A cup full of foaming wine — the wicked drink to the dregs." Horns cut and raised.

hebtext.note: "You say, ‘I choose the appointed time (mōʿēd); it is I who judge with equity’" (v.2). God speaks directly: HE chooses when to judge. When the earth quakes, HE holds its pillars firm (v.3). The warning to the arrogant: "do not lift your horns against heaven" (v.5). God’s timing is sovereign; human posturing is futile.

"In the hand of the LORD is a cup (kōs) full of foaming wine mixed with spices; he pours it out, and all the wicked of the earth drink it down to its very dregs" (v.8). The cup of judgment: not a sip but drain it to the dregs. The image returns in Revelation 14:10. The psalm closes: "I will cut off the horns of all the wicked, but the horns of the righteous will be lifted up" (v.10). Horns lowered and raised by divine decree.

themes.note: Judgment and Sovereignty peak: God chooses the time, holds the pillars, pours the cup. Justice: horns cut off and raised. Prophecy: the cup of wrath (Rev 14:10; Matt 26:39).

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'We praise you, God, for your Name is near,' consider asking God whether nearness — not answers, not relief, just His presence — is enough for you in this season.
